sections.title: 1. The Preparation of Soups
introduction: The nutritious liquids known under the name of Soups are of comparatively recent origin. Indeed, as they are now served, they do not date any further back than the early years of the nineteenth century.

The soups of old cookery were, really, complete dishes, wherein the meats and vegetables used in their preparation were assembled. They, moreover, suffered from the effects of the general confusion which reigned in the menus of those days. These menus seem to have depended in no wise, for their items, upon the progressive satisfaction of the consumers’ appetites, and a long procession of dishes was far more characteristic of the meal than their judicious order and diversity.

In this respect, as in so many others, Carême was the reformer, and, if he were not, strictly speaking, the actual initiator of the changes which ushered in our present methods, he certainly had a large share in the establishment of the new theories.

Nevertheless, it took his followers almost a century to bring soups to the perfection of to-day, for modern cookery has replaced those stodgy dishes of yore by comparatively simple and savoury preparations which are veritable wonders of delicacy and taste. Now, my attention has been called to the desirability of drawing up some sort of classification of soups, if only with the view of obviating the absurdity of placing such preparations as are indiscriminately called Bisque, Purée, Cullis, or Cream under the same head. Logically, each preparation should have its own special formula, and it is impossible to admit that one and the same can apply to all.

It is generally admitted that the terms Veloutés and Creams, whose introduction into the vocabulary of cookery is comparatively recent, are peculiarly well suited to supplant those of Bisque and Cullis, which are steadily becoming obsolete, as well as that too vulgar term Purée. Considerations of this kind naturally led me to a new classification of soups, and this I shall disclose later.

I shall not make any lengthy attempt here to refute the arguments of certain autocrats of the dinner-table who, not so many years ago, urged the total abolition of soups. I shall only submit to their notice the following quotation from Grimod de la Reynière, one of our most illustrious gastronomists: “Soup is to a dinner what the porch or gateway is to a building,” that is to say, it must not only form the first portion thereof, but it must be so devised as to convey some idea of the whole to which it belongs; or, after the manner of an overture in a light opera, it should divulge what is to be the dominant phrase of the melody throughout.

I am at one with Grimod in this, and believe that soups have come to stay. Of all the items on a menu, soup is that which exacts the most delicate perfection and the strictest attention, for upon the first impression it gives to the diner the success of the latter part of the meal largely depends.
Soups should be served as hot as possible in very warm plates, especially in the case of consommés when these have been preceded by cold hors-d’œuvres.
Hors-d’œuvres are pointless in a dinner, and even when oysters stand as such they should only be allowed at meals which include no soup.

Those hors-d’œuvres which consist of various fish, smoked or in oil, and strongly seasoned salads, leave a disagreeable taste on the consumer’s palate and make the soup which follows seem flat and insipid if the latter be not served boiling hot.

sections.title: Classification of Soups
introduction: This includes (1) clear soups, (2) thick soups, (3) special soups of various kinds, (4) classical vegetable soups, including some local preparations.

entries.title: CLEAR SOUPS
entries.introduction: Clear soups, of whatever nature the base thereof may be, whether butcher’s meat, poultry, game, fish, shell-fish, or turtle, &c., are made according to one method only. They are always clear consommés to which has been added a slight garnish in keeping with the nature of the consommé.

entries.title: THICK SOUPS
introduction: These are divided into three leading classes as follows:—(1) The Purées, Cullises, or Bisques. (2) Various Veloutés. (3) Various Creams.
notes: Though the three preparations of the first class are practically the same, and, generally speaking, the Cullises and the Bisques may be considered as purées of fowl, game, or shell-fish, it is advisable to distinguish one from another by giving each a special name of its own.

Thus the word Purée is most suitably applied to any preparation with a vegetable base. The term Cullis is best fitted to preparations having either poultry, game, or fish for base, while bisque, in spite of the fact that in former days it was applied indiscriminately to purées of shell-fish, poultry, pigeons, &c., distinctly denotes a purée of shell-fish (either lobster, crayfish, or shrimp, &c.).
In short, it is imperative to avoid all ambiguities and to give everything its proper name, or, at least, that name which identifies it most correctly.

entries.title: PURÉES
introduction: Farinaceous vegetables, such as haricot-beans and lentils, and the floury ones, such as the potato, need no additional thickening ingredient, since the flour or fecula which they contain amply suffices for the leason of their purées.
On the other hand, aqueous vegetables like carrots, pumpkins, turnips, celery, and herbs cannot dispense with a thickening ingredient, as their purées of themselves do not cohere in the least.

instructions.Cohering or Thickening Elements; their Q: In order to effect the coherence of vegetable purées, either rice, potato, or bread-crumb cut into dice and fried in butter may be used. The proportion of these per pound (454 g) of vegetables should be respectively 3 oz (85 g), 10 oz (283 g), and 10 oz (283 g).
instructions.The Dilution of Purées: Generally this is done by means of ordinary white consommé, though in certain cases, as, for instance, if the soup is a Lenten one, milk is used.
instructions.The Finishing: When the purées have been strained and brought to the required consistence they should be boiled and stirred. Then they are placed on the side of the fire to simmer for twenty-five or thirty minutes. It is at this stage that they are purified by means of the careful removal of all the scum that forms on their surface. When dishing up complete them, away from the fire, with 3 oz (85 g) of butter per quart (946 ml) of soup, and pass them once more through a strainer.
instructions.Purée Garnishes: These are usually either small fried crusts, small dice of potato fried in butter, a chiffonade, some kind of little brunoise, or, more generally, chervil pluches.

entries.title: CULLISES
entries.introduction: Cullises have for their base either poultry, game, or fish.

Thickening Ingredients: For fowl, 2 or 3 oz (57-85 g) of rice, or 0.75 pint (355 ml) of poultry velouté per lb (454 g) of fowl.
For game, 3 or 4 oz (85-113 g) of lentils, or 0.75 pint (355 ml) of game Espagnole per lb (454 g) of game.
For fish, a clear panada made up of French bread soaked in boiling salted milk. Use 5 oz (142 g) of bread and 1 good pint (473 ml) of milk per lb (454 g) of fish.
instructions.Finishing: Having strained and made up the Cullises, boil them while stirring (except in the case of fish cullises, which must not boil, and must be served as soon as they are made), then place them in a bain-marie and butter their surfaces lest a skin should form. At the last moment complete them with 2 or 3 oz (57-85 g) of butter per quart (946 ml).
Garnish: The garnish of poultry or game cullises consists of either small dice of game or fowl-fillets, which should be kept aside for the purpose; a fine julienne of these fillets, or small quenelles made from the latter, raw.
The garnish of fish cullis is generally fish-fillets poached in butter and cut up into small dice or in julienne-fashion.

entries.title: BISQUES
instructions: The invariable base of Bisques is shell-fish cooked in mirepoix. (Recipe 228)

Their thickening ingredients are rice, fish velouté, or crusts of bread fried in butter, the proportion being 3 oz (85 g) of rice, 10 oz (283 g) of bread-crusts, or 0.75 pint (355 ml) of fish velouté per lb (454 g) of shell-fish cooked in mirepoix. (Recipe 228)
When the soup is strained, treat it in precisely the same way as the cullises. (Recipe 240)
The garnish consists of small dice of the meat from the shell-fish used. These pieces should have been put aside from the first.

entries.title: THE VELOUTÉS
entries.introduction: These differ from the purées, cullises, and bisques in that their invariable thickening element is a velouté whose preparation is in harmony with the nature of the ingredients of the soup, these being either vegetables, poultry, game, fish, or shell-fish.

instructions.The Preparation of the Velouté: Allow 3.5 oz (99 g) of white roux per quart (946 ml) of the diluent. This diluent should be ordinary consommé for a velouté of vegetables or herbs, chicken consommé for a poultry velouté, or very clear fish fumet for a fish or shell-fish velouté. The procedure is exactly the same as that described under Recipe 25 of the leading sauces.
instructions.The Apportionment of the Ingredients: In general, the quantities of each constituent are in the following proportion:—Velouté, one-half; the purée of the substance which characterises the soup, one-quarter; the consommé used to bring the soup to its proper consistence, one-quarter. In respect of finishing ingredients, use, for thickening, the yolks of 3 eggs and 0.2 pint (95 ml) of cream per quart (946 ml) of soup.
Rules Relative to the Preparation: If the velouté is to be of lettuce, chicory, celery, or mixed herbs, these ingredients are scalded for five minutes, drained, gently stewed in butter, and added to the prepared velouté in which their cooking is completed.
If carrots, turnips, onions, &c., are to be treated, finely mince them, stew them in butter without allowing them to acquire any colour, and add them to the velouté.
If fowl be the base, cook it in the velouté. This done, withdraw it, remove the meat, finely pound same, and add it to the velouté, which is then rubbed through tammy.
In the case of fish the procedure is the same as for fowl.
For game, roast or sauté the selected piece, bone it, finely pound the meat, and combine the latter with the velouté, which should then be rubbed through tammy.
For shell-fish, cook these in a mirepoix, finely pound them together with the latter, add to the velouté, and pass the whole through tammy.
instructions.The Completing of Velouté: Having passed the soup through tammy, bring it to its proper degree of consistence with the necessary quantity of consommé, boil while stirring, and place in a bain-marie. At the last moment finish the soup with the leason and 2 oz (57 g) of butter per quart (946 ml) of liquid.
Garnish for Velouté: For vegetables: Chiffonade, fine printaniers, or brunoise.
For fowl and game: The fillets of one or the other, poached and cut into small dice or in julienne-fashion; little quenelles made with the raw fillets, or either fowl or game royales.
For fish: Small dice or fine julienne of fish fillets poached in butter.
For shell-fish: Small dice of cooked shell-fish meat put aside for the purpose.

notes: In certain circumstances these garnishes are increased by means of 3 tbsp (45 ml) of poached rice per quart (946 ml) of the soup.

entries.title: THE CREAMS
entries.introduction: Practically speaking, the preparation of the creams is the same as that of the veloutés, but for the following exceptions:
instructions: 1. In all circumstances, clear Béchamel is substituted for velouté.
2. The correct consistence of the soup is got by means of milk instead of consommé.
3. Creams do not require egg-yolk leasons.
4. They are not buttered, but they are finished with 0.2 or 0.4 pint (95 or 189 ml) of fresh cream per quart (946 ml).
Creams allow of the same garnishes as the veloutés. (Recipe 242)

entries.title: SPECIAL SOUPS AND THICKENED CONSOMMÉS
entries.introduction: These are of different kinds, though their preparation remains the same, and they do not lend themselves to the requirements of veloutés or creams. I should quote as types of this class the Ambassador, à l’Américaine, Darblay, Faubonne, &c. The same holds good with thickened consommés, such as “Germiny,” “Coquelin,” &c.

entries.title: VEGETABLE SOUPS
introduction: These soups, of which the “Paysanne” is the radical type, do not demand very great precision in the apportionment of the vegetables of which they are composed; but they need great care and attention, notwithstanding.
The vegetables, in the majority of cases, must undergo a long stewing in butter, an operation the object of which is to expel their vegetable moisture and to saturate them with butter.
In respect of others which have a local character, the vegetables should be cooked with the diluent, without a preparatory stewing.

entries.title: FOREIGN SOUPS
entries.introduction: In the course of Part II. of this work I shall allude to certain soups which have a foreign origin, and whose use, although it may not be general, is yet sufficiently common. If only for the sake of novelty or variety, it is occasionally permissible to poach upon the preserves of foreign nations; but apart from this there exist among the recipes of foreigners many which can but enrich their adopter, besides being generally appreciated.

sections.title: 2. Braising, Poaching, Sautés, and Poëling
introduction: Except for the roasts, grills, and fryings, which will be discussed later, all culinary operations dealing with meat are related to one of the four following methods: Braising, poëling, poaching, and sautés.
These four methods of cooking belong, however, to the sauces, and this explains how it is that the latter hold such a pre-eminent position in French cookery.

Before devoting any attention to particular formulæ, which will be given in the second part of this work, it seemed desirable to me to recapitulate in a general way the theory of each of these cooking methods. These theories are of paramount importance, since it is only with a complete knowledge of them that good results may be obtained by the culinary operator.

entries.title: ORDINARY BRAISINGS
entries.introduction: Of all the various culinary operations, braisings are the most expensive and the most difficult. Long and assiduous practice alone can teach the many difficulties that this mode of procedure entails, for it is one which demands extraordinary care and the most constant attention. Over and above the question of care and that of the quality of meat used, there are also these conditions to be fulfilled in order that a good braising may be obtained, namely, that excellent stock should be used in moistening, and that the braising base be well prepared.

instructions.Meats that are Braised: Mutton and beef are braised in the ordinary way. The best for the purpose is that derived from an animal of 3 to 6 years of age in the case of beef, and 1 to 2 years in the case of mutton.
instructions.The Larding of Meats for Braising: Meats like rumps or leg of mutton that are not sufficiently fat are larded with square strips of bacon fat (about 0.5 inch or 1.27 cm thick). These strips are seasoned with pepper, nutmeg, spices, parsley, and marinaded for two hours in brandy. Insert them into the meat equidistantly using larding needles. The proportion of fat to meat should be about 3 oz (85 g) per lb (454 g).
instructions.To Marinade Braisings: Season meats with salt, pepper, and spices. Place them in a receptacle between two litters of aromatics, cover them with white or red 'vin ordinaire' in the proportion of 0.25 pint (118 ml) per lb (454 g) of meat, and leave them to marinade for about six hours, turning them three or four times.
instructions.The Aromatics or Base of the Braising: These are thickly sliced and fried carrots and onions, in the proportion of 1 oz (28 g) per lb (454 g) of meat, one faggot, including one garlic clove and 1.5 oz (43 g) of fresh, blanched bacon-rind.
To Fry, Prepare, and Cook Braised Meat: Drain the marinaded meat and wipe it dry. Heat clarified fat in a thick saucepan and, when hot, put the meat in to acquire colour on all sides.
Withdraw the meat, cover it with slices of larding-bacon if it is lean, and string it.
Pour the marinade into the braising-pan, and place the meat on a litter of the vegetables the marinade contained. Cover the pan and rapidly reduce the wine to a syrup.
Add sufficient brown stock to cover the meat, set to boil, and then put it in a moderate oven. Cook until a braiding needle may be deeply pricked without any blood being drawn. (First Phase)
Transfer the meat to another clean utensil. Then choose one of the following:

1. For a clear liquor: Strain the liquor over the meat through muslin. Place the pan in the oven to complete cooking, basting from time to time. Thicken the liquor with arrow-root as for an ordinary thickened gravy. (Recipe 41)

2. For a sauce: Reduce the liquor to half before returning to the meat. Restore its volume by means of two-thirds of its quantity of Espagnole sauce and one-third of tomato purée. Complete the cooking in this sauce, basting as before. When cooked, withdraw the meat, strain the sauce, let the grease settle, and carefully remove it. Rectify the sauce's consistency if necessary.
instructions.The Glazing of Braised Meat: Place the cooked meat in the front of the oven, sprinkle it slightly with its cooking liquor, and push it into the oven so this liquor may dry and form a glaze. Repeat this operation eight or ten times.

notes: The explanations on the theory of braising, including the two phases of cooking and the critique of the 'pinçage' method, are lengthy but crucial for understanding the process. The first phase concentrates juices in the center of the meat, while the second phase involves the disaggregation of tissues and the exchange of juices between the meat and the sauce.

entries.title: BRAISING OF WHITE MEATS
entries.introduction: White braisings are made with the neck, the saddle, the loin, the fillets, the fricandeaus, and the sweet-bread of veal, young turkeys and fat pullets, and sometimes, relevés of lamb. The procedure is the same for all these meats; the time of cooking alone varies. The aromatics are the same as those of the brown braisings, but the frying of them is optional. The moistening liquor is brown veal stock. (Recipe 9)
instructions: Except for veal sweet-bread, which is always blanched, the meats may be slightly stiffened and browned in butter. This is not essential.
Place them in a utensil just large enough to hold them, with aromatics underneath.
Moisten with a little veal stock, set to boil on a moderate fire, and reduce the stock with the lid on.
When this stock has assumed the consistency of a glaze, add a further similar quantity of fresh stock, and reduce as before.
The third time, moisten the veal until it is half covered, and push the pan into a moderate oven.
Baste constantly while it cooks.
Braised white meat is known to be cooked when, after being deeply pricked, it exudes an absolutely colourless liquid.
White braised meats are generally glazed.

entries.title: POACHINGS
introduction: The best possible definition of a poaching is a boiling that does not boil. The term extends to all slow processes of cooking which involve the use of a liquor, however small.

The unalterable principle is that the poaching liquor must not boil, though it should reach a degree of heat as approximate as possible to boiling-point. Another principle is that large pieces of fish or poultry be set to boil in cold liquor. All other preparations whose mode of cooking is poaching gain by being immersed in liquor which has reached the required temperature beforehand.

Poaching Poultry: Properly prepare the piece of poultry and truss it. If stuffing, do so before trussing. If larding, rub with lemon and dip the relevant portions in boiling white stock to stiffen the skin.
Place the poultry in a receptacle just large enough to hold it, and moisten with excellent white stock.
Set to boil, skim, put the lid on, and continue the cooking at a low simmer.
The fowl is known to be cooked when, after pricking the thick of the leg close to the 'drumstick', the issuing liquid is white.

notes: The need for a properly sized receptacle is to ensure the piece is wholly immersed and that the resulting sauce is concentrated and flavourful.
The white stock used should be prepared beforehand and be very clear. Cooking the poultry with the raw stock ingredients would result in a savourless sauce as the cooking times do not align.

entries.title: POËLINGS
entries.introduction: Poëlings are, practically speaking, roasts, for the cooking periods of each are the same, except that the former are cooked entirely or almost entirely with butter.

Preparation of Poëled Meats: Place a layer of raw matignon in the bottom of a deep and thick receptacle. (Recipe 227)
Place the seasoned meat or poultry on the vegetables and sprinkle copiously with melted butter.
Cover the utensil, and push it into a moderately hot oven. Cook gently, like a stew, and frequently sprinkle with melted butter.
When cooked, uncover the utensil so the meat may acquire a fine colour. Then transfer to a dish and keep covered.
Add a sufficient quantity of brown veal stock to the vegetables in the pan. (Recipe 9) Set to boil gently for ten minutes.
Strain through a serviette, carefully remove all grease, and serve the stock in a sauceboat with the meat.
Special Poëlings known as 'En Casserole': These are actual poëlings cooked and served in special earthenware utensils.

'En casserole' preparations are simply cooked in butter, without vegetables. When cooked, the piece is withdrawn, excellent brown veal stock is poured into the utensil, simmered, and skimmed of butter before the piece is returned. (Recipe 9)

'En cocotte' preparations are the same, but the piece is garnished with vegetables (mushrooms, artichoke bottoms, small onions, etc.) which are half-cooked in butter before being added to complete their cooking with the meat.

notes: It is of paramount importance that poëlings not be moistened during cooking, except for feathered game like pheasants, which may have a small quantity of burnt brandy added when nearly cooked.
Do not remove the grease from the vegetables before the moistening stock is added, as the butter contains much of the flavour.

entries.title: THE SAUTÉS
entries.introduction: What characterises the process we call “sauté” is that the object treated is cooked dry—that is to say, solely by means of a fatty substance such as butter, oil, or grease. All products treated in this way must be frizzled—that is to say, they must be put into the fat when it is very hot in order that a hardened coating may form around them which will keep their juices within.

instructions.Fowl and Game Sautés: After frizzling, complete cooking on the stove or in the oven with the lid off, basting with butter like a roast. Withdraw the pieces to swill the utensil, then return them to the sauce or garnish just to warm through.
instructions.Red Meat Sautés (tournedos, cutlets, etc: Always effected on the stove in a small quantity of clarified butter. The thinner the piece, the more rapid the frizzling. When blood appears on the raw side, turn it over. When drops of blood appear on the second side, it is cooked.
instructions.White Meat Sautés (veal, lamb): Must also be frizzled in hot fat, but their cooking must be completed gently on the side of the fire, often with the lid on.
instructions.Swilling the Utensil: After removing the product, remove the grease and pour a condimentary liquid (like wine) into the saucepan. Set to boil to dissolve the solidified gravy, and add this liquid to the prepared sauce or garnish.

sections.title: Roasts
introduction: Of the two usual methods of roasting, the spit will always be used in preference to the oven, if only on account of the conditions under which the operation is effected, and whatever be the kind of fuel used—wood, coal, or gas.

The reason of this preference is clear if it be remembered that, in spite of every possible precaution during the progress of an oven roast, it is impossible to avoid an accumulation of vapour around the cooking object in a closed oven. And this steam is more particularly objectionable inasmuch as it is excessive in the case of delicately flavoured meats, which latter are almost if not entirely impaired thereby.

The spitted roast, on the contrary, cooks in the open in a dry atmosphere, and by this means retains its own peculiar flavour. Hence the unquestionable superiority of spitted roasts over the oven kind, especially in respect of small feathered game.

In certain circumstances and places there is no choice of means, and, nolens volens, the oven has to be used; but, in this case at least, all possible precautions should be observed in order to counteract the effects of the steam above mentioned.

entries.title: LARDING BACON FOR ROASTS
introduction: Poultry and game to be roasted ought generally to be partly covered with a large thin slice of larding bacon, except those pieces of game which in special cases are larded.

The object and use of these slices are not only to shield the fillets of fowl and game from the severe heat of the fires but also to prevent these from drying while the legs, which the heat takes much longer to penetrate than the other parts, are cooking. The slices of bacon should therefore completely cover the breasts of fowl and game, and they should be tied on to the latter by means of string.
In some cases roasts of butcher’s meat are covered with layers of veal- or beef-fat, the object of which is similar to that of the bacon prescribed above.

entries.title: SPITTED ROASTS
introduction: The whole theory of roasts on the spit might be condensed as follows:—

In the case of butcher’s meat, calculate the intensity of the heat used according to the piece to be roasted, the latter’s size and quality, and the time it has hung. Experience, however, is the best guide...
The three following rules will be found to cover all the necessary directions for spitted roasts:

1. All red meats containing a large quantity of juice should be properly set, and then, according to their size, made to undergo the action of a fire capable of radiating a very penetrating heat with little or no flame.
2. In the case of white meats, whose cooking should be thorough, the fire ought to be so regulated as to allow the roast to cook and colour simultaneously.
3. With small game the fuel should be wood, but whatever fuel be used the fire ought to be made up in suchwise as to produce more flame than glowing embers.

entries.title: OVEN ROASTS
introduction: The degree of heat used for each roast must be regulated according to the nature and size of the latter after the manner of spitted roasts.

An oven roast, in the first place, should always be placed on a meatstand, and this should be of such a height that at no given moment during the cooking process the meat may come in contact with the juices and fat which have drained from it into the utensil beneath. Failing a proper stand, a spit resting upon the edges of the utensil may be used.

No liquid of any kind, gravy or water, need be put in the baking-pan. The addition of any liquid is rather prejudicial than otherwise, since by producing vapour which hangs over the roast it transforms the latter into a stew.
notes: Whether spitted or in the oven, a roast must always be frequently basted with a fatty substance, but never with any other liquid.

entries.title: THE GRAVY OF ROASTS
instructions: The real and most natural gravy for roasts is made from the swilling of the baking- or dripping-pan. To obtain this, neither the utensils nor the gravy ought to have burned.

When a greater quantity is required, prepare a stock beforehand from bones and trimmings of a similar nature to the roast. Place these in a pan with fat and roast them. Transfer to a saucepan, moisten to cover with tepid, salted water, add the swillings of the roasting pan, boil, skim, and cook gently for 3-4 hours. Strain and put aside for swilling.

instructions.Swilling: Having removed the roast, take off a portion of the grease from the pan, and pour into it the required quantity of prepared gravy. Reduce by half, strain, and almost entirely remove grease.

notes: It is a mistake to remove all the grease from, and to clarify, the gravy of roasts. Treated thus they are certainly clearer and more sightly, but a large proportion of their savour is lost, and it should be borne in mind that the gravy of a roast is not a consommé.

entries.title: THE DRESSING AND ACCOMPANIMENTS OF ROASTS
introduction: As a rule, a roast ought not to wait. It ought only to leave the spit or oven in order to be served. All roasts should be placed on very hot dishes, slightly besprinkled with fresh butter, and surrounded by bunches of watercress (this is optional). The gravy is invariably served separately.
Roasts of butcher’s meat and poultry are dished up as simply as possible.
Small roasted game may be dished up on fried slices of bread-crumb masked with gratin stuffing. (Recipe 202)
When lemons accompany a roast, they should be served separately.
The mediæval custom of dishing game with the plumage has been abandoned.
Roast feathered game à l’anglaise is dished up with or without potato chips, and the three adjuncts are gravy, bread-crumbs, and bread-sauce.
In northern countries game roasts are always accompanied either by slightly sugared stewed apples, or by cherry or apricot jam.

sections.title: Grills
introduction: Those culinary preparations effected by means of grilling belong to the order called cooking by concentration. And, indeed, in almost all cases, the great object of these operations, I might even say the greatest object, is the concentration, in the centre, of the juices and essences which represent, most essentially, the nutritive principles of the products cooked.
A grill, which is, in short, but a roast on an open fire, stands, in my opinion, as the remote starting-point, the very genesis of our art.

instructions.Fuel for Grills: The best is live coal or small pieces of charcoal. It is essential that it produce no smoke.
instructions.The Bed of Charcoal: The arrangement must be regulated according to the size and kind of the products. It should be set in equal layers in the centre, and slightly raised on the sides.
instructions.The Grill: The grill must always be placed over the glowing fuel in advance, and it should be very hot when the objects to be grilled are placed upon it, otherwise they would stick.

entries.title: RED MEAT GRILLS
entries.introduction: I submit as a principle that the golden rule in grills is to strictly observe the correct degree of heat which is proper to each treated object, never forgetting that the larger and richer in nutrition the piece of meat, the quicker and more thorough must be its initial setting.
instructions: For large pieces of meat (beef or mutton), the better the quality, the more resisting must be the rissoled coating they receive. The fierceness of the fire should be proportionately abated the moment the initial rissoling has been effected.
For thinner pieces, a quick rissoling of their surfaces over a fierce fire, and a few minutes of subsequent cooking will be all they need.
Before placing meats on the grill, baste them slightly with clarified butter, and repeat this frequently during cooking.
Grilled red meat should always be turned by means of special tongs. Do not pierce the surface.
Cooking is complete when the meat resists pressure from a finger, or when little beads of blood appear upon the rissoled surface.

entries.title: WHITE-MEAT GRILLS
entries.introduction: Superficial rissoling is not as necessary as with red meats. For this kind of grills keep a moderate fire, so that the cooking and colouring of the meat may take place simultaneously.
instructions: Baste fairly often with clarified butter while cooking, lest their outsides dry.
They are known to be cooked when the juice issuing from them is quite white.

entries.title: FISH GRILLS
instructions: Use a moderate fire and only grill after having copiously sprinkled them with clarified butter or oil. Sprinkle them similarly while cooking.
A grilled fish is cooked when the bones are easily separated from the meat.
Except for fatty fish, always roll fish to be grilled in flour before sprinkling with melted butter. This gives them a golden external crust and keeps them from drying.

entries.title: THE GRILLING OF PRODUCTS COATED WITH BUTTER AND BREAD-CRUMBS
entries.introduction: These grills generally consist of only small objects; they must be effected on a very moderate fire, with the view of enabling them to cook and acquire colour simultaneously. They should also be frequently besprinkled with clarified butter, and turned with care, so as not to break their coating, the object of which is to withhold their contained juices.

entries.title: FRYINGS
introduction: Frying is one of the principal cooking processes. Its procedure is governed by stringent laws and rules which it is best not to break.

Utensils used in frying should be made of copper or other resisting metal; they should be in one piece, oval or round, and sufficiently large and deep to allow, while only half-filled with fat, of the objects being properly affected by the latter.
Utensils with vertical sides are preferable to those with slanting kind.

entries.title: FRYING FAT—ITS PREPARATION
introduction: Any animal or vegetable grease is suitable for frying, provided it be quite pure and possess a resisting force allowing it to reach a very high temperature without burning. But for frying on a large scale, the use of cooked and clarified fats, such as the fat of “pot-au-feu” and roasts, should be avoided.
The fat of kidney of beef generally forms the base of the grease intended for frying on a large scale.
The grease used for frying ought not only to be melted down, but also thoroughly cooked, so that it may be quite pure.
instructions: Cut the grease into pieces and then put it into the saucepan with 1 pint (473 ml) of water per every 10 lbs (4.5 kg).
The grease is cooked when the membranes are converted into greaves and it gives off smoke with a distinct smell.
At this stage, remove it from the fire for about ten minutes to cool, then strain it through a sieve or a coarse towel.

entries.title: THE VARIOUS DEGREES OF HEAT REACHED BY THE FRYING MEDIUM, AND THEIR APPLICATION
entries.introduction: The temperature reached by a frying medium depends upon its constituents and purity. The various degrees may be classified as moderately hot, hot, very hot.

instructions.Testing Temperature: It is moderately hot when a sprig of parsley begins to bubble immediately; it is hot if it crackles when a slightly moist object is thrust into it; it is very hot when it gives off a thin white smoke.
instructions.Moderately Hot (275°-284°F / 135°-140°C): Used for all products containing vegetable water and for fish that require cooking by penetration before concentration.
instructions.Hot (320°F / 160°C): Used for all products that have undergone initial cooking, and for products that must be set immediately, such as those panés à l’anglaise or covered with batter (croquettes, fritters, etc.).
instructions.Very Hot (356°F / 180°C): Used for all objects that need a sharp and firm setting, and for all small objects whose cooking is effected in a few minutes, as in the case of whitebait.

entries.title: FRYING MEDIUM FOR FISH
instructions: Oil is best suited to the frying of fish, especially the very small kind.
The temperature should be regulated strictly in accordance with the size of the fish to be fried, in order that its cooking and colouring may be effected simultaneously.
Except Nonats and whitebait, which are simply rolled in flour, fish to be fried are previously steeped in slightly salted milk and then rolled in flour.
When finished, fried fish are drained, dried, slightly salted, and dished on a serviette or on paper, with a garnish of fried parsley-sprays and sections of channelled lemon.

entries.title: THE QUANTITY OF THE FRYING MEDIUM
entries.introduction: This should always be in proportion to the quantity or size of the objects to be fried, bearing in mind that these must always be entirely submerged. The greater the amount of fat, the higher the temperature reached, and the less one need fear a sudden cooling of the fat when the objects are immersed.

entries.title: THE CARE OF THE FRYING MEDIUM
entries.introduction: Every time a frying fat is used it should, after having been melted, be strained through a towel, for the majority of objects which it has served to cook must have left some particles behind them which might prove prejudicial to the objects that are to follow. Not only do these foreign substances disturb the clearness of the fat and render it liable to burn, but they are exceedingly detrimental to the objects that are treated later.

entries.title: GRATINS
entries.introduction: The various kinds of the order 'Gratins' are (1) the Complete Gratin; (2) the Rapid Gratin; (3) the Light Gratin; (4) Glazing, which is a form of Rapid Gratin.

entries.title: COMPLETE GRATIN
entries.introduction: This is the first example of the series; its principal constituent must be completely cooked, and its cooking must be coincident with the reduction of the sauce and the formation of the gratin proper.
instructions: The base is almost invariably ordinary or Lenten duxelle sauce. (Recipe 223)
The object to be treated is laid on a buttered dish, surrounded with slices of raw mushrooms and chopped shallots, and covered with duxelle sauce.
The surface is then sprinkled with raspings, and copiously moistened with melted butter.
When withdrawing the gratin from the oven squeeze a few drops of lemon-juice over it, and besprinkle it with chopped parsley.
notes: 1. If too much sauce is used, the object will cook before the sauce reduces.
2. If the sauce is insufficient, it will reduce before the object is cooked.
3. The larger the piece, the more moderate the heat should be. Conversely, the smaller it is, the fiercer the fire should be.

entries.title: RAPID GRATIN
instructions: Proceed as above, with duxelle sauce, but the products treated are always cooked and warmed in advance.
Cover the object with the necessary quantity of sauce, besprinkle with raspings and butter, and set the gratin to form in a fierce oven.

entries.title: LIGHT GRATIN
entries.introduction: This is proper to farinaceous products, such as macaroni, lazagnes, noodles, gnocchi, &c., and consists of a combination of grated cheese, raspings, and butter. A moderate heat is all that is wanted. Also considered as light gratins are those which serve as the complement of stuffed vegetables such as tomatoes, mushrooms, egg-plant, and cucumber.

entries.title: GLAZINGS
entries.introduction: These are of two kinds—they either consist of a heavily buttered sauce, or they form from a sprinkling of cheese upon the sauce.
instructions: In the first case, after pouring sauce over the object, place the dish on another dish containing a little water. The greater the quantity of butter used, the more intense the heat required, in order that a slight golden film may form almost instantaneously.

In the second case, the sauce used is always a Mornay. (Recipe 91) Cover the object with the sauce, besprinkle with grated cheese and melted butter, and place in fairly intense heat, so that a slight golden crust may form.

entries.title: BLANCHINGS
entries.introduction: The term blanchings is applied to three classes of operations:

instructions.1. The blanching of meats: This obtains mostly in the case of calf’s head and foot, sweet-bread, and trotters. These meats are first set to soak in cold, running water. They are then placed on the fire in a saucepan with enough cold water to cover, and the water is gradually brought to the boil. Boiling time varies by item. As soon as blanched, the meats are cooled in fresh water.
instructions.2. The blanching, or parboiling of certa: This term is wrongly applied to the cooking of green vegetables like French beans, which is more correctly termed 'à l’anglaise'.
instructions.3. The blanching of certain other vegeta: This consists in partly cooking vegetables in plenty of water to rid them of any bitter or pungent flavour. This is chiefly resorted to for lettuce, chicory, endives, celery, artichokes, cabbages, and out-of-season root vegetables. After blanching, these vegetables are always cooled in cold water.

entries.title: THE TREATMENT OF DRY VEGETABLES
instructions: It is wrong to soak dry vegetables if they are of good quality. They need only be put into a saucepan with enough cold water to cover, and with 1 oz (28 g) of salt per 5 quarts (4.7 L) of water.
Set to boil gently, skim, add the aromatic garnish (carrots, onions, faggot), and cook gently with lid on.
notes: If the vegetables are old or inferior, they might be put to soak in soft water for about 1.5 hours. A prolonged soaking may give rise to incipient germination.

entries.title: BRAISED VEGETABLES
instructions: Vegetables to be braised must be first blanched, cooled, pared, and strung.
Garnish the bottom of a saucepan with blanched pork-rind, sliced carrots and onions, and a faggot. Cover the sides with thin slices of bacon.
Lay the vegetables upon the litter, and leave them to sweat in the oven for about ten minutes with lid on.
Now moisten enough to cover with white stock, and set to cook gently.
Drain, remove string, and cut to the shape required. Lay them in a sautépan, and cover them with their reduced stock from which the grease has been removed.
If prepared in advance, put them aside in basins, cover them with their cooking-liquor (strained, boiling, and with grease), and cover with buttered paper.

entries.title: LEASON OF GREEN VEGETABLES WITH BUTTER
instructions: First thoroughly drain the vegetables and toss them over the fire for a few minutes to rid them of moisture.
Season, add the butter away from the fire, and slightly toss, rolling the saucepan to mix the butter with the vegetables.

entries.title: LEASON OF VEGETABLES WITH CREAM
instructions: Vegetables must be kept somewhat firm. After draining, put them into a saucepan with enough boiling fresh cream to well moisten without covering them.
Finish their cooking process in the cream, stirring occasionally.
When the cream is almost entirely reduced, finish, away from the fire, with a little butter.
The leason may be slightly stiffened with a few tablespoonfuls of cream sauce if necessary.

entries.title: VEGETABLE CREAMS AND PURÉES
instructions: Purées of dry and farinaceous vegetables may be obtained by rubbing them through a sieve. Put the purée into a sautépan, dry it over a brisk fire, adding 1.5 oz (43 g) of butter per pint (473 ml) of purée; then add milk or cream until the required consistence is reached.
For purées of aqueous vegetables, such as French beans, cauliflowers, celery, &c., a quarter of their volume of mashed potatoes should be added to them to effect their leason.
In the case of vegetable creams, substitute for the mashed potatoes an equivalent quantity of succulent and stiff Béchamel sauce.

entries.title: GARNISHES
introduction: In cookery, although garnishes only play a minor part, they are, nevertheless, very important, for, besides being the principal accompaniments to dishes, they are very often the adornment thereof, while it frequently happens that their harmonious arrangement considerably helps to throw the beauty of a fine joint or bird into relief.
A garnish may consist of one or more products. Be this as it may, its name, as a rule, distinctly denotes, in a word, what it is and how it is made.
In any case, it should always bear some relation to the piece it accompanies, either in the constituents of its preparation or with regard to the size of the piece constituting the dish.

The constituents of garnishes are supplied by vegetables, farinaceous products, quenelles of all kinds, cocks’ combs and kidneys, truffles and mushrooms, plain or stuffed olives, molluscs (mussels or oysters), shell-fish (crayfish, shrimps, lobster, &c.), butcher’s supplies, such as lamb’s sweet-bread, calf’s brains, and calf’s spine-marrow.

As a rule, garnishes are independent of the dish itself—that is to say, they are prepared entirely apart. At other times they are mixed with it, playing the double part of garnish and condimentary principle, as in the case of Matelotes, Compotes, Civets, &c.

sections.title: PART II - RECIPES AND MODES OF PROCEDURE
introduction: In Part I. of this work I treated of the general principles on which the science of cookery is founded, and the leading operations constituting the basis of the work.
In Part II. I shall proceed from the general to the particular—in other words, I shall set forth the recipes of every dish I touch upon, its method of preparation, and its constituent parts.

With the view of making reference as easy as possible, without departing from a certain logical order, I have adopted the method of classifying these recipes in accordance with the position the dishes they represent hold in the ordinary menu, and thus, starting with the hors-d’œuvres, I go straight on to the dessert. I was compelled, however, to alter my plan in the case of eggs, which never appear on the menu of a dinner save in Lent.
These I have therefore placed immediately after the hors-d’œuvres, which, like eggs, should only be served at luncheons, for reasons I shall explain later.

It will be seen that I have placed the Savouries before the Entremets, instead of after the Ices, as is customary in England. My reason for this apparent anomaly is that I consider it a positive gastronomical heresy to eat fish, meats, fowl-remains, &c., after delicate Entremets and Ices, the subtle flavour of the latter, which form such an agreeable item in a dinner, being quite destroyed by the violent seasoning of the former.

Moreover, the very pretext brought forward in support of this practice, so erroneous from the gastronomical standpoint, namely, “that after a good dinner it is necessary to serve something strange and highly seasoned, in order to whet the diner’s thirst,” is its own condemnation.

For, if appetite is satiated and thirst is quenched, it follows that the consumer has taken all that is necessary. Therefore, anything more that he may be stimulated to take will only amount to excess, and excess in gastronomy, as in everything else, is a fault that can find no excuse.

At all events, I could agree to no more than the placing of the Savouries before mild Entremets, and, even so, the former would have to consist of light, dry preparations, very moderately seasoned, such as Paillettes with Parmesan, various kinds of dry biscuits, and small tartlets garnished with cheese soufflé.
In short, if I expressed my plain opinion on the matter, I should advise the total suppression of Savouries in a dinner.
